The **Cooks River**, a semi-mature [tide](/wiki/Tide "Tide")-dominated [drowned valley estuary](/wiki/Ria "Ria"),[[1]](#cite_note-NSWDEH-1) is a [tributary](/wiki/Tributary "Tributary") of [Botany Bay](/wiki/Botany_Bay "Botany Bay"), located in southern [Sydney](/wiki/Sydney "Sydney"), [New South Wales](/wiki/New_South_Wales "New South Wales"), Australia.

The course of the 23-kilometre-long (14 mi) urban waterway has been altered to accommodate various developments along its shore. It serves as part of a [stormwater](/wiki/Stormwater "Stormwater") system for the 100 square kilometres (39 sq mi) of its watershed, and many of the original streams running into it have been turned into concrete lined channels. The tidal sections support significant areas of [mangrove ecosystems](/wiki/Mangrove_forest "Mangrove forest") and are used for recreational activities.

## Course

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cooks_River&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Course")]

|  |  |
| --- | --- |
| [icon](/wiki/File:Question_book-new.svg) | This section **does not [cite](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citing_sources "Wikipedia:Citing sources") any [sources](/wiki/Wikipedia:Verifiability "Wikipedia:Verifiability")**. Please help [improve this section](/wiki/Special:EditPage/Cooks_River "Special:EditPage/Cooks River") by [adding citations to reliable sources](/wiki/Help:Referencing_for_beginners "Help:Referencing for beginners"). Unsourced material may be challenged and [removed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Verifiability#Burden_of_evidence "Wikipedia:Verifiability"). *(September 2023)* *([Learn how and when to remove this message](/wiki/Help:Maintenance_template_removal "Help:Maintenance template removal"))* |

The river begins at Graf Park, [Yagoona](/wiki/Yagoona "Yagoona"), then flows in a roughly north-easterly direction to [Chullora](/wiki/Chullora "Chullora"). It reaches its northernmost point at [Strathfield](/wiki/Strathfield "Strathfield"), where it leads into a [concrete](/wiki/Concrete "Concrete") open canal, no more than one metre wide and thirty centimetres deep. It then heads towards the south-east. Where Cooks River runs through Strathfield Golf Course, the concrete lining has been partly removed. Here the plants have returned and have created an environment where the water is filtered and runs clean, and where wildlife has returned. One section here is called the Chain of Ponds.

At [Belfield](/wiki/Belfield,_New_South_Wales "Belfield, New South Wales") it joins with the [Cox Creek Channel](/wiki/Coxs_Creek_(Belfield,_New_South_Wales) "Coxs Creek (Belfield, New South Wales)") and flows in an easterly direction. The canal widens and deepens as it picks up stormwater from surrounding suburbs, such as [Campsie](/wiki/Campsie,_New_South_Wales "Campsie, New South Wales") and becomes influenced by tidal action.

At [Canterbury](/wiki/Canterbury,_New_South_Wales "Canterbury, New South Wales"), it is joined by [Cup and Saucer Creek](/wiki/Cup_and_Saucer_Creek "Cup and Saucer Creek"). Industrial areas line the Cooks River at Canterbury. In the past, factories discharged their waste directly into the water.

The [valley](/wiki/Valley "Valley") becomes more pronounced as the river reaches [Tempe](/wiki/Tempe,_New_South_Wales "Tempe, New South Wales"), where it is joined by [Wolli Creek](/wiki/Wolli_Creek "Wolli Creek") and the [Alexandra Canal](/wiki/Alexandra_Canal_(New_South_Wales) "Alexandra Canal (New South Wales)"). The [Princes Highway](/wiki/Princes_Highway "Princes Highway") crosses the Cooks River and links Tempe to the suburb of [Wolli Creek](/wiki/Wolli_Creek,_New_South_Wales "Wolli Creek, New South Wales"). Also here is the low-lying [Fatima Island](/wiki/Fatima_Island "Fatima Island"), which is submerged at high tide.

The course of the final south-flowing section of river is entirely artificial. The river formerly flowed, via marshes, on a meandering course across the present site of [Sydney Airport](/wiki/Sydney_Airport "Sydney Airport") to meet Mill Stream and enter [Botany Bay](/wiki/Botany_Bay "Botany Bay") from the northern end of the bay. The boundary between the [Parish of Botany](/wiki/Parish_of_Botany "Parish of Botany") and the [Parish of St George](/wiki/Parish_of_St_George "Parish of St George") remains at the former mouth of Cooks River. This part of the river course was altered to accommodate the expanding Sydney Airport. Cooks River now connects with [Botany Bay](/wiki/Botany_Bay "Botany Bay") at [Kyeemagh](/wiki/Kyeemagh "Kyeemagh").

The corridor of land beside the river is a conduit for many services along large parts of its length including electricity, high-voltage lines, a high-pressure oil pipe owned by [Shell](/wiki/Shell_plc "Shell plc"), and high-volume sewage pipes. Many of the lower-lying areas have been filled and are parks and sports grounds. In spite of this the river and adjacent lands are being recognised for their beauty, history, amenity and environmental value, if not also for potential for improvements. The walkway and [cycleway](/wiki/Bike_Paths_in_Sydney#Bay_to_Bay_.28Homebush_to_Cronulla_and_Kurnell.29 "Bike Paths in Sydney") beside much of the river is part of the path connecting [Sydney Olympic Park](/wiki/Sydney_Olympic_Park "Sydney Olympic Park") with Botany Bay.

## Management

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cooks_River&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Management")]

Care and control of the river is shared between many [local councils](/wiki/Local_government_in_Australia#New_South_Wales "Local government in Australia") and the New South Wales Government through agencies such as the Sydney Metropolitan Catchment Management Authority, the [Department of Planning & Environment](/wiki/Department_of_Planning_%26_Environment "Department of Planning & Environment") and [Sydney Water](/wiki/Sydney_Water "Sydney Water").

The river also has a number of community-based organisations which assist its maintenance, in particular the Cooks River Valley Association (CRVA). The CRVA grew from The Cooks River Improvement League, founded in 1925, which had published a book entitled "Our Ocean to Ocean Opportunity". The League recommended cleaning up the river by developing a canal system and removing of the footings of Tempe Dam, proposal which had been made originally by engineer H B Henson, in 1896.

In 2010 the Cup and Saucer Wetlands were built in Canterbury to create a natural environment for the native wildlife and fauna.[[2]](#cite_note-2)

Selected areas of the Cooks River which is lined with concrete will be replaced in 2013 and 2014 with a more natural bank with sandstone rocks and indigenous planting.[[3]](#cite_note-3)

## Environmental issues

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cooks_River&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Environmental issues")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e1/2nd_leg%2C_on_this_Air_Canada_plane_-_Sydney_to_Vancouver%2C_BC_-_Sydney_subburbs_and_beaches_from_the_air_%2813113706185%29.jpg/250px-2nd_leg%2C_on_this_Air_Canada_plane_-_Sydney_to_Vancouver%2C_BC_-_Sydney_subburbs_and_beaches_from_the_air_%2813113706185%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:2nd_leg,_on_this_Air_Canada_plane_-_Sydney_to_Vancouver,_BC_-_Sydney_subburbs_and_beaches_from_the_air_(13113706185).jpg)

Aerial view of the river and surrounding suburbs

The Cooks River has a catchment area of 100 square kilometres (39 sq mi), within the metropolitan areas of Sydney enclosed by urban environments and extensive recreational areas. Over 400,000 people live in this area and there are over 100,000 commercial and industrial premises. The river has typical water catchment management problems characteristic of heavily urbanised areas, including:

* Removal of native [vegetation](/wiki/Vegetation "Vegetation") and [habitat destruction](/wiki/Habitat_destruction "Habitat destruction")
* Modified [hydrology](/wiki/Hydrology "Hydrology") including increased peak run-off rates due to covering of land with hard surfaces, concrete storm water canals and re-routing of water courses
* Pollution from motor vehicles, litter, sewage, illegal dumping, industrial, commercial and domestic activities
* [Nutrient](/wiki/Nutrient "Nutrient") enrichment from surrounding environment, especially due to water runoffs from recreational parks, resulting in increased [algal](/wiki/Algal "Algal") levels

The [Cooks River Alliance](http://cooksriver.org.au) is a partnership between councils in the Cooks River Catchment. It works with the local communities to restore the health of the river. The Alliance Board is made up of one councillor from each member council. The members of the Cooks River Alliance are: [Bayside](/wiki/Bayside_Council "Bayside Council"), [Canterbury-Bankstown](/wiki/City_of_Canterbury-Bankstown "City of Canterbury-Bankstown"), [City of Sydney](/wiki/City_of_Sydney "City of Sydney"), [Inner West](/wiki/Inner_West_Council "Inner West Council") and [Strathfield](/wiki/Municipality_of_Strathfield "Municipality of Strathfield"). The Alliance combines and builds resources, experience, knowledge and skills within councils and communities, to work on complex environmental challenges. Community Representatives will have the opportunity to contribute to the direction of the Alliance, and provide local resident perspectives.

Water quality testing conducted by the Water Research Centre found quantities of pharmaceutical and industrial chemicals at levels similar to untreated sewage.[[4]](#cite_note-srgos-4) A very old and broken sewerage system has been blamed for the contaminants which included soaps, insecticides, caffeine and anticonvulsants.[[4]](#cite_note-srgos-4)

A group of local Councils fund the project named Riverlife, which trains tour guides and organises interpretive walking and cycling tours of the "river" most weekends.[[5]](#cite_note-Tebbutt-5)

In 2022, educator and YouTuber [Beau Miles](/wiki/Beau_Miles "Beau Miles") attempted to paddle a kayak down the full length of the Cooks River, producing a documentary of the journey to highlight the poor condition of the river, which he called the "sickest urban river in Australia".[[6]](#cite_note-6)

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cooks_River&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: History")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/00/Cooks_river_dam_kiln_1871_V1-FL1226619.tif/lossy-page1-250px-Cooks_river_dam_kiln_1871_V1-FL1226619.tif.jpg)](/wiki/File:Cooks_river_dam_kiln_1871_V1-FL1226619.tif)

Lime kiln and dam, Cooks River ca. 1875

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d1/SLNSW_796183_Cooks_River_Campsie.jpg/250px-SLNSW_796183_Cooks_River_Campsie.jpg)](/wiki/File:SLNSW_796183_Cooks_River_Campsie.jpg)

View of the Cooks River at Campsie ca. 1900-1927

Before European Settlement, the [Indigenous Australian](/wiki/Indigenous_Australian "Indigenous Australian") population used the river by [fishing](/wiki/Fishing "Fishing") and gathering shellfish. This had little effect on the river's natural [ecology](/wiki/Ecology "Ecology") as sustainable practices were adhered to. It is generally thought that four different clans, each speaking a distinct dialect of the Darug language, inhabited the area surrounding the river. The [Bediagal](/wiki/Bediagal "Bediagal") clan occupied the area on the southern side of the river. whilst on the northern side, it is generally thought that the Gameygal clan lived between the mouth of the Cooks River and the ocean. Further west, the river was used by the [Cadigal](/wiki/Cadigal "Cadigal") people (whose country extended north up to Port Jackson and took in the area where modern-day Sydney is now located). Further west still and along the upper reaches of the river, the country was occupied by the Wangal people.

In 1770, [Captain Cook](/wiki/Captain_Cook "Captain Cook") sailed into [Botany Bay](/wiki/Botany_Bay "Botany Bay") and made the first written description of the river as follows: "I found a very fine stream of fresh water on the north side in the first sandy cove within the island before which a ship might lay land-locked and wood for fuel may be got everywhere."[[7]](#cite_note-7) The existence of the river appeared to make settlement a possibility; however, when the [First Fleet](/wiki/First_Fleet "First Fleet") arrived the river and valley was regarded as unsuitable. Captain [John Hunter](/wiki/John_Hunter_(Royal_Navy_officer) "John Hunter (Royal Navy officer)") and Lieutenant Bradley both mentioned the shallowness of the water and large [swamp](/wiki/Swamp "Swamp") areas. In July 1791, [Watkin Tench](/wiki/Watkin_Tench "Watkin Tench") and [William Dawes](/wiki/William_Dawes_(British_Marines_officer) "William Dawes (British Marines officer)") explored south of [Rose Hill](/wiki/Rosehill,_New_South_Wales "Rosehill, New South Wales") (modern [Parramatta](/wiki/Parramatta "Parramatta")) in search of a large river, which they described as a “second [Nile](/wiki/Nile "Nile") or [Ganges](/wiki/Ganges "Ganges").” They found instead a tidal [saltwater](/wiki/Saltwater "Saltwater") creek that drained into Botany Bay, likely part of the Cooks River or its tributaries, and noted the lack of [fresh water](/wiki/Fresh_water "Fresh water") for drinking along its banks.[[8]](#cite_note-8) The first land grants along the river tended to be fairly large and used mostly for [grazing](/wiki/Grazing "Grazing") and [timber](/wiki/Timber "Timber") with some fishing and lime burning at Botany Bay. [Governor Macquarie](/wiki/Governor_Macquarie "Governor Macquarie") makes reference to a slender bridge in his 1810 [diary](/wiki/Diary "Diary"), adding that "the soil is bad and neither good for tillage or pasturage". Nevertheless, some farmers did find they could till the land and settlement along the river spread, and roads and crossings were made in several places.

In the 1830s a [dam](/wiki/Dam "Dam") was built across the river; however, this did not provide a clean water supply and the structure itself began to damage the river's ecology by preventing tidal flushing of deposited urban silt. A second dam was built to serve a [sugar mill](/wiki/Sugar_mill "Sugar mill") at the new village of [Canterbury Vale](/wiki/Canterbury,_New_South_Wales "Canterbury, New South Wales"). The location took advantage of the water supply and [barge](/wiki/Barge "Barge") transport. The mill closed in 1855; however, other polluting industries were later to follow including [wool](/wiki/Wool "Wool") washes, [tanneries](/wiki/Tanning_(leather) "Tanning (leather)") and [boiling down](/wiki/Boiling_down "Boiling down") works. Despite the increasingly doubtful quality of the water, the river remained a popular place in the late nineteenth century for [boating](/wiki/Boat "Boat"), [picnics](/wiki/Picnic "Picnic") and [swimming](/wiki/Human_swimming "Human swimming"). In 1894, artist [Sydney Long](/wiki/Sydney_Long "Sydney Long") painted an idyllic scene of boys swimming in the Cook's River, entitled *By Tranquil Waters*, which was so well received that it was bought by the Trustees of the [Art Gallery of New South Wales](/wiki/Art_Gallery_of_New_South_Wales "Art Gallery of New South Wales").[[9]](#cite_note-9)[[10]](#cite_note-10) By the 1880s tree clearing resulted in [erosion](/wiki/Erosion "Erosion"), [silting](/wiki/Silting "Silting") and expansion of [reed beds](/wiki/Reed_beds "Reed beds"), blocking river flow.

On the weekend of 25–27 May 1889, 425 millimetres (16.7 in) of rain [inundated](/wiki/Inundate "Inundate") all the low-lying land. At the height of the flood, the top of the Sugarworks Dam was covered by 3 metres (10 ft) of water.[[11]](#cite_note-11)[[12]](#cite_note-12) Authorities responded to the extensive damage and loss of animals by removing the dams and weirs. A proposed series of canals connecting the river with Sydney Harbour was not commenced. A sewage farm operated near the mouth of the river from 1889 to 1916. A railway was used in both the construction of mains and filtration buildings, as well as in the day-to-day operation, moving screened solids throughout the farm.[[13]](#cite_note-13)

Concreting of the river banks commenced in the 1930s and the Cooks River Improvement Act of 1946 confirmed the policy of the tidying up of an undisciplined stream within neat [cement](/wiki/Cement "Cement") boundaries. Between 1947 and 1953, engineers diverted the original [river mouth](/wiki/River_mouth "River mouth") to the west of [Sydney Airport](/wiki/Sydney_Airport "Sydney Airport") to accommodate new runways.[[14]](#cite_note-14)

Initiatives from 1976 onwards have attempted to preserve and return the natural features of the river system with tree planting, pollution traps and [landscaping](/wiki/Landscaping "Landscaping"). [Footpaths](/wiki/Footpath "Footpath") and a cycle track were built to increase recreational use of the river. The NSW State Government committed A$2.9 million to the restoration of the sheet piling on the banks of the river.[[5]](#cite_note-Tebbutt-5) In November 2007 the Federal [Australian Labor Party](/wiki/Australian_Labor_Party "Australian Labor Party"), then in opposition, made an announcement of A$2 million for environmental projects on the Cooks River.[[15]](#cite_note-15)

In 2009, Strathfield Council established the [Cooks River Fun Run](https://web.archive.org/web/20140512214245/http://www.cooksriverfunrun.com.au/index.html). This event is held annually around June or July each year. The event starts at Freshwater Park Strathfield, with a 5 km and 10 km run, a 5 km walk and 2 km kids' run.
